params.text:
What is COSHH?

COSHH stands for the Control of Substances Hazardous to Health Regulations 2002. It is the law that requires employers to prevent or control workers' exposure to substances that can harm their health. In a care setting it protects you, your colleagues, the people you support and visitors from harm caused by everyday products and materials.

Many people picture chemicals in a factory when they hear "hazardous substances", but hazards are all around us in social care. A bottle of thick bleach under the sink, the disinfectant used to clean a commode, the clinical waste in a yellow bag and even dust from a dry mop can all cause ill health if they are used, stored or disposed of carelessly.

  • COSHH covers chemicals, products containing chemicals, fumes, dusts, vapours, mists, gases and biological agents such as bacteria and viruses.
  • It does not cover asbestos, lead or radioactive substances, which have their own separate regulations.
  • The aim is simple: identify what could cause harm, then put sensible measures in place so that harm does not happen.

Why COSHH matters in care

Ill health caused by hazardous substances is largely preventable. Poor practice can lead to skin conditions such as dermatitis from repeated contact with cleaning products, breathing problems from inhaling fumes, chemical burns from splashes, or the spread of infection from bodily fluids.

The Care Quality Commission (CQC) expects services to keep people safe, and the safe management of hazardous substances is part of providing safe, well-led care. Following COSHH is not just a legal duty under the Health and Safety at Work etc. Act 1974 and the COSHH Regulations – it is a core part of good, dignified care.

params.question: COSHH only applies to industrial chemicals, so it has nothing to do with everyday care work.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: Not quite. COSHH applies to a wide range of substances found in care settings every day, not just industrial chemicals.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct. COSHH covers many everyday substances in care, including cleaning products, disinfectants, clinical waste and bodily fluids.
